title: Conscious_Consumer Study Notes
text:
Crafted with <3 by abhirsc :)


Use this project in this order:

Start from the user flow.
Read the data file shape.
Read the sync script.
Read the page that renders the table.
Run the offline test before touching live data.

This project is easiest to understand if you think in layers:

Input layer: Tally collects form responses.
Sync layer: Python maps responses into CSV rows.
Content layer: reviews.csv becomes the source of truth for reviews.
Presentation layer: Quarto reads the CSV and renders the table on the website.






The site is static. A static site cannot directly write to a server-side database during page rendering, so the project stores reviews in reviews.csv. That file acts like a lightweight table.



Tally stores submissions in its own system. The Python sync script translates Tally response fields into the exact column names the site expects.



The file .github/tally_state.json remembers the last processed submission. This prevents duplicated rows when the scheduled workflow runs again.



The Quarto page reads reviews.csv and filters rows where Status == "Approved". This gives a simple moderation gate before public display.





index.qmd: renders the public review table.
reviews.csv: the table data source.
scripts/sync_tally_reviews.py: fetches and maps Tally responses.
.github/workflows/sync-tally.yml: runs the sync on a schedule.
.github/tally_state.json: stores deduplication state.






Run the script with the sample payload first:
cd /Users/abhirsc/Documents/Conscious_Consumer
python3 scripts/sync_tally_reviews.py \
  --responses-file tests/data/sample_tally_responses.json \
  --dry-run



Set these first:

TALLY_API_KEY
TALLY_FORM_ID

Then run:
cd /Users/abhirsc/Documents/Conscious_Consumer
python3 scripts/sync_tally_reviews.py \
  --csv-path reviews.csv \
  --state-file .github/tally_state.json




Think of the project as:
form submission -> normalized record -> CSV row -> rendered website table
If something breaks, check it in that same order.

title: Product Blog Library
text:
Welcome to the blog section! Here you’ll find deeper dives into standout products, comparisons between brands, and eco tips for smarter shopping in Sydney.
Whether you’re curious about bamboo toothbrushes or deciding between reusable cups, this is where we break it all down.

title: 🪥 Are Bamboo Toothbrushes Worth It?
text:
Bamboo toothbrushes are one of the easiest eco swaps, but not all are created equal. Let’s explore the good, the bad, and the biodegradable 🌱



Compostable handles
Stylish and minimal packaging
Widely available in Sydney eco stores





Some use nylon bristles (not compostable)
May feel rough for sensitive gums
Price varies widely




Brand: BHoomi
Eco Score: 4/5
Recommended: Yes
Review: “Great texture and feel. Love the simplicity.”


Want to share your experience with a different brand? Submit your review here

title: 🕶️ Product Review & Sustainability Report: Bambies Daintree Glasses
text: The Bambies Daintree glasses are lightweight, beautifully crafted eyewear made with FSC-certified walnut and maple wood. With a smooth natural finish and spring-loaded hinges, they fit snugly and look unique thanks to the natural wood grain. They are compatible with prescription lenses, making them both fashionable and practical.

title: 🕶️ Product Review & Sustainability Report: Bambies Daintree Glasses
text: The Bambies Daintree glasses are lightweight, beautifully crafted eyewear made with FSC-certified walnut and maple wood. With a smooth natural finish and spring-loaded hinges, they fit snugly and look unique thanks to the natural wood grain. title: 🕶️ Product Review & Sustainability Report: Bambies Daintree Glasses
text:
🧭 Sustainability Scorecard (Rated out of 5)








Category
Score
Notes




Materials
5/5
FSC-certified wood, bio-based resin finish, and plant cellulose lenses


Packaging
4/5
Minimal and recyclable, no overpackaging


Manufacturing Ethics
3.5/5
Sustainably sourced wood; no info on labor standards or carbon offset


Durability
4.5/5
Long-lasting, replaceable lenses, good build


End-of-Life Impact
3/5
Biodegradable frame, but no take-back or recycling scheme offered



🔎 Overall Sustainability Rating: 4.2 / 5

title: 🕶️ Product Review & Sustainability Report: Bambies Daintree Glasses
text:
📝 Personal Experience
I’ve been a Bambies customer for over three years and have owned three pairs:
1. Daintree – my absolute favorite. I loved the feel, the look, and the vibe. Sadly, I lost it in Brisbane’s Mowbray Park.
2. Another pair I ordered had a small defect, but Bambies replaced it efficiently and without fuss. Their customer service really shines.
3. My current pair? Loving it again. Still fits perfectly, lightweight, and draws compliments.
As someone who values sustainable design, good craftsmanship, and honest service — I keep coming back.

title: 🕶️ Product Review & Sustainability Report: Bambies Daintree Glasses
text:
💡 Suggestions for Improvement

Offer a lens recycling or frame return program (circular economy bonus!)

Include more transparency about carbon footprint or ethical sourcing details

Maybe consider limited-edition collaborations with conservation orgs

title: 🕶️ Product Review & Sustainability Report: Bambies Daintree Glasses
text:
📸 Photos
Photos to be added soon — including my current pair and hopefully one day a reunited Daintree!

title: All Reviews
text:
Lets move towards true goods and filter out all greenwashing with the power of community.


Welcome to our community-powered review hub. Discover quick, trustworthy feedback from locals on eco-friendly products available in Sydney, then add your own review in under a minute.


Scan or click the QR card to submit a review, then browse what the community has approved.



  

Scan or click to submit your review


Fast feedback from your phone helps keep the table current.
